interrogator_dossier:
# Aptiv PLC (APTV) — Interrogator Forensic Dossier

*Transcripts: Q1 2025 (2025-05-01) through Q1 2026 (2026-05-05), six consecutive quarters spanning the run-up to and completion of the Electrical Distribution Systems (EDS) spin-off into Versigent (VGNT, NYSE), distributed April 1, 2026.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story over these six quarters is a deliberate, well-telegraphed **conglomerate-to-pure-play transformation** executed against a deteriorating auto-production backdrop. It moved through three phases:

**Phase 1 — "Resilient operator weathering the storm" (Q1–Q2 2025).** The opening narrative was defensive and macro-dominated: tariffs, USMCA compliance, China EV-customer mix, FX (the Mexican peso). Kevin Clark led every call with the "in-region, for-region" supply-chain digital twin as the moat. Revenue was flat-to-down ("Revenue totaled $4.8 billion, down 1%," Q1-25), and the entire pitch was *operating execution offsetting external headwinds* — SG&A down 10% in 2024, targeted another 5% in 2025, "more aggressive in terms of looking for opportunities to further reduce costs" (Q1-25). The EDS separation was mentioned as "on track" but was background.

**Phase 2 — "The spin becomes the story" (Q3 2025–Q4 2025).** As 2025 progressed, the separation moved to center stage. By Q4-25 (2026-02-02) management had named the entity ("Versagen," CEO Joe Liatine), set the date ("spin out... on April 1"), and quantified the stranded-cost drag ($70M called out at Investor Day, "$50 million impact in the first full year on a pro forma basis" for NewAptiv plus "~$15 million" public-company setup for the SpinCo). Simultaneously the segment architecture was relabeled: "Advanced Safety and User Experience" became **"Intelligent Systems"** (Q4-25), reframing the legacy ADAS/infotainment business as a software-and-edge-compute growth engine.

**Phase 3 — "New Aptiv: intelligence at the edge" (Q1 2026).** Post-separation (completed April 1, 2026), Clark's prepared remarks were "focused exclusively on New Aptiv." The narrative pivoted hard from *auto-supplier-managing-decline* to *diversified software/edge-compute platform*: "sense, think, act and continually optimize," ~25% non-automotive revenue, Wind River ($6B TAM), and a brand-new **robotics/drones** growth vector (partnerships with Robust AI, Vecna Robotics, Comau). The center of gravity shifted from cost-out and tariffs to TAM expansion and bookings ($7B total awards Q1-26; $20B+ NewAptiv 2026 bookings target).

**Growth drivers introduced:** robotics/cobots/AMRs and drones (new in Q4-25/Q1-26); Wind River edge-AI ecosystem (steadily amplified, "over 20%" growth); non-auto end markets (A&D, telecom, data centers, industrial — "rapidly approaching almost $4 billion" by Q3-25). **Drivers quietly de-emphasized:** the original tariff/USMCA crisis framing (Q1-Q2 2025's dominant topic) faded once it proved "de minimis"; the China-local-OEM-mix-headwind, hammered for four straight quarters, was reframed in Q1-26 as turning "positive growth... in Q2."

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

The headline tension: management claimed **"record" results every single quarter** (record Q-revenue, OI, EPS) while the actual fiscal-year financials show a company whose net income collapsed from $2.94B (2023) to $165M (2025) and EPS from $10.39 to $0.75. The reconciliation is that management speaks almost exclusively in **adjusted, ex-amortization, ex-restructuring, ex-special-items, ex-FX/commodity** terms — and the gap between that scorecard and GAAP reality is the central forensic story.

| Management claim | Quarter | Hard financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "record... earnings per share of $2.17" / "$1.86" / "record EPS" | Q3-25, Q4-25 | FY2025 GAAP EPS **$0.75**, down **-89.2% YoY**; 5-yr EPS CAGR **-21.1%** | **Diverges sharply.** Adjusted "records" are driven heavily by an 18–20% share-count reduction (the $3B ASR completed Apr-1-2025), not earnings growth. The denominator, not the numerator, makes the records. |
| "strong operating performance... flow-through on volume" | every Q | FY net margin **0.8%**; ROIC **6.8%** (below WACC); Gross margin 19.1% (expanding) | **Partial.** Gross-margin expansion and the SG&A program are real and corroborated. But net margin near zero and sub-cost-of-capital ROIC say operating strength is being consumed below the line (amortization, interest on $6.2B net debt, restructuring). |
| "$818M / $584M / $510M operating cash flow... accelerate deleveraging" | Q2–Q4-25 | TTM FCF **$1.09B** (down -5.4% YoY but 3-yr CAGR +54%); P/FCF 9.8x; Net debt **$6.18B** | **Largely supported.** Cash generation is genuine and the deleveraging claim is credible. This is the strongest pillar — FCF is the real asset here, not accounting EPS. |
| "$31 billion in new business awards this year" | Q1–Q3-25 (reaffirmed) | Delivered **$27B** (Q4-25: "short of our target of $31 billion") | **Missed.** A clean, dated guidance miss — the first hard credibility dent. Management blamed timing ("customer awards shifting to 2026"), which is plausible but is exactly the deflection analysts flagged all year. |
| "confident in reaching... over $31 billion" → silently lowered | Q1-26 | NewAptiv 2026 bookings guide **"$20 billion"** (not comparable post-spin) | **Reset, not missed.** The bar moved with the perimeter; reasonable, but the serial "timing shift" language is now a pattern. |
| Margin "expansion of 30 basis points, excluding FX and commodities" | Q1-26 | Margin **-1.65σ vs historical peak**; Net margin 0.8% | **The ex-FX qualifier is load-bearing.** On a reported basis margins compressed (Q4-25 Intelligent Systems margins were "surprisingly weak," -170bps FX hit). The "ex-FX/commodity" framing is honest as a same-store metric but flatters a business taking real FX losses. |

**Net read:** the cash story (FCF, deleveraging) **matches** the numbers; the earnings story is **richer than the trajectory justifies** because it leans on adjustments and a shrinking share count to manufacture "records" atop a -89% GAAP EPS collapse.

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: STABLE-to-mildly-defensive, with a confident pivot in the final quarter.** Management's confidence is remarkably consistent — almost scripted — but the *content* of that confidence shifted from offense to defense and back:

- **Q1-25:** Cautious-defensive. "we're currently in a period of uncertainty due to rapid changes in global trade policies"; explicitly **declined to guide** H2 ("we don't have the visibility to forecast revenue for Q3 and Q4"). Confident on cost control, evasive on demand.
- **Q2-25:** Steady. "record second quarter results," but flagged "ongoing roll-off of legacy user experience programs" and "slowdown... on select Zeekr and NIO programs in China... headwinds for the next few quarters" — *proactively naming* future weakness (a green-flag honesty signal).
- **Q3-25:** Most confident of the year offensively. "$8.4 billion of new business awards," raised full-year guidance — but immediately hedged on Nexperia and trade ("we just overlaid some incremental conservatism").
- **Q4-25:** Mixed. Confident on the spin and bookings, but had to own the $31B miss and explain "surprisingly weak" Intelligent Systems margins (Colin Langan's words, conceded by Clark). Defensive on FX/peso.
- **Q1-26:** Re-energized, offensive. Full "intelligence at the edge" investment-thesis framing, robotics TAM, "$20 billion of bookings... high level of confidence." The tone reset to growth/investment language precisely as the perimeter narrowed to the higher-margin RemainCo.

**Specificity over time:** Management got *more* specific on bookings and segment detail but persistently *less* specific (deliberately) on forward revenue, repeatedly refusing full-year/half-year guidance in 2025 ("we're not providing guidance for the second half"). The shift from "offensive" (TAM, robotics, edge AI) to "defensive" (SG&A -5%, tariff mitigation, FX hedges) and back maps cleanly to the macro cycle and the spin timeline.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM-HIGH on the controllables, MEDIUM on the externals.**

- **Earnings-beat rate: 7/7 (100%)** per the screener, corroborated by the Q1-26 print ($1.71 vs $1.62 consensus). This is a genuine, durable sandbagging-and-deliver pattern on EPS — management consistently sets a beatable bar and clears it, helped by the buyback tailwind.
- **The one hard miss:** the **$31B bookings target → $27B actual** (Q4-25). They reaffirmed "$31 billion" in Q1, Q2, and Q3 ("we continue to expect roughly $31 billion") before conceding the shortfall. The miss was ~13%, blamed entirely on timing. Credibility cost: real but limited, because bookings are a softer KPI than EPS and the "shifted to 2026" explanation is at least internally consistent (2026 guide is $20B+ for the smaller NewAptiv).
- **Cost/cash guidance: reliably delivered.** The SG&A reduction program (-10% 2024, -5% 2025) and the deleveraging/buyback cadence were stated and executed (ASR completed on schedule, 20% of shares retired over 12 months).
- **Pattern:** Sandbag EPS, deliver; over-promise bookings, under-deliver once; execute cost/capital plans precisely. Net: **High** on EPS and capital allocation, **Medium** on commercial momentum (bookings).

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **China mix / growth-over-market (every quarter, the dominant recurring pressure).** Colin Langan (Wells Fargo) pressed it in Q1, Q2, and again on Versagen EV exposure in Q4. Management's answers were consistently directional and non-quantified — "I'm not sure we'll give you an exact dollar amount on the headwind" (Q3-25). The Zeekr/NIO program cancellations (named Q2-25) became the bad news that the earlier "we're on track to match production mix" optimism had soft-pedaled. **This is the clearest deflect-then-deliver-bad-news pattern.**
- **Second-half visibility (2025).** Langan in Q1-25 walked management into the implied H2 acceleration ("a pretty major step up in growth over market") and Clark simply refused: "we're not providing guidance for the second half." Analysts wanted a forecast; management gave a framework.
- **In-source vs outsource ADAS (Q2-25, Tom Narayan/RBC).** A strategic-risk probe — do large OEMs pull ADAS in-house? Management answered confidently ("that trend has reversed course"), which is a *claim about competitors' behavior* that bears watching.
- **Versagen stranded costs and pro-forma EBITDA bridge (Q4-25, James Picariello).** Sharp, technical pressure on the $75M EBITDA gap, stranded costs ($70M), and standup costs — management answered cleanly, a green flag.
- **Intelligent Systems margin weakness (Q4-25, Langan).** "surprisingly weak" — management conceded three drivers (FX -170bps, engineering-credit timing, robotics investment). Honest, but it confirmed the segment's margins are lumpy and FX-exposed.

**What analysts worry about that management isn't fully addressing:** whether the bookings-to-revenue conversion is real (timing slips two years running), and whether non-auto/robotics is a genuine margin mix-shifter or an investment sink ("incremental investment... will continue into 2026," Q4-25).

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **GAAP earnings collapse masked by "record" adjusted EPS.** FY2025 GAAP EPS $0.75 (-89%) vs a steady drumbeat of "record" adjusted quarters — the gap is bridged by adjustments + an 18–20% share-count reduction. The "records" are partly financial engineering.
🔴 **Serial bookings "timing shifts."** The $31B → $27B miss, blamed on slippage to 2026, then a 2026 guide that itself notes awards "could shift." A two-year pattern of moving the goalposts forward.
🔴 **Insider conviction is negative.** Open-market reality: **2 buys (9,800 sh) vs 15 sells (113,488 sh)** — the grant-inflated buy-ratio in the scan is misleading; the real signal is net selling. No insider is backing up the truck on the "New Aptiv" re-rate story.
🔴 **China growth-over-market chronically negative**, with program cancellations (Zeekr/NIO) that the prior optimism had not flagged; "down 14%" in NewAptiv China Q1-26.
🔴 **FX/peso is a structural, recurring OI drag** management cannot fully hedge (peso from ~21 to sub-18; "a ton of OI impact"), and Versagen carries the un-hedged operational exposure.

🟢 **Free-cash-flow generation is genuine and improving** ($818M OCF in Q4-25; TTM FCF $1.09B; 3-yr FCF CAGR +54%), funding both buybacks and deleveraging — the real asset.
🟢 **The spin was executed on time and as promised** (Versigent/VGNT, April 1, 2026) — management did exactly what it said over four quarters, a credibility deposit.
🟢 **Proactive bad-news disclosure** (named Zeekr/NIO roll-offs and legacy-program decline *before* they fully hit) and clean answers to technical stranded-cost questions.
🟢 **100% EPS beat rate (7/7)** and a Piotroski 8/9 — operationally the business is well-run and financially clean despite the headwinds.
🟢 **Real cost discipline** (SG&A -10% then -5%, seven facilities consolidated) — the controllables are managed tightly.

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **The segment rename is the tell.** "Advanced Safety and User Experience" → **"Intelligent Systems"** (Q4-25). This is not cosmetic; it reframes a business carrying a *declining* legacy infotainment/UX roll-off as a forward-looking edge-compute platform, conveniently as the spin approached and the equity story needed a growth label.
- **Two different SpinCo names appear in the transcripts** — "Versagen" (Q4-25, Q1-26 prepared remarks) and the SEC/market name **"Versigent" (ticker VGNT)** — and even "Versigent"/"Versagen" used loosely in the Q1-26 transcript. The transcript's "Versigent" closing-day reference matches the filed entity. (Cosmetic, but worth noting the transcript transcription is imperfect.)
- **Capital-allocation language shifted from buybacks to a fuller toolkit.** Early 2025 was dominated by the completed $3B ASR (defensive, EPS-supportive). By Q1-26 the framing is "allocated both organically and inorganically... while also returning capital" — i.e., M&A is back on the table for NewAptiv. Watch for a bolt-on acquisition in software/robotics, which would be a use-of-cash pivot.
- **New voice — Joe Liatine (Versagen CEO)** appears in Q4-25; his presence signals the SpinCo is a genuine operating entity, not a financial shell. The robotics partnerships (Robust AI, Vecna, Comau) are new commercial relationships introduced only in the last two quarters — an embryonic, unproven optionality layer being sold as a growth pillar.
- **Competition framing softened on in-sourcing risk** ("OEMs... less desire to do things internally," Q2-25) precisely when that risk was most topical — a confident assertion that, if wrong, undercuts the entire ADAS bull case.

---

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING (structurally complete) → STABLE post-spin.**

Aptiv has just finished a clean, well-executed conglomerate-to-pure-play pivot: the low-margin, copper-indexed, peso-exposed EDS commodity-wiring business was spun out as Versigent (VGNT) on **April 1, 2026**, leaving a higher-margin "New Aptiv" of Intelligent Systems + Engineered Components with ~25% non-auto revenue and a nascent robotics/edge-AI optionality layer. The execution was credible; the FCF is real; the cost discipline is real.

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** *the load-bearing structural catalyst (the EDS spin) has ALREADY FIRED — the re-rate is spent, not pending.* What remains is a **post-spin "show-me" margin-and-mix story** that requires the market to re-rate a cyclical Tier-1 auto supplier with **0.8% net margins, -89% GAAP EPS, sub-WACC ROIC (6.8%), $6.2B net debt, negative open-market insider conviction, and chronic China-mix headwinds** on the *promise* of software/robotics diversification. The segment-revenue block in the screener still includes EDS (41% of revenue) and is therefore **stale** for any go-forward sum-of-parts — value only the two remaining segments. Per peer comps, APTV screens **rich, not cheap**, vs Lear/Magna/BorgWarner (6–8x P/FCF) despite the optically low 9.8x raw multiple. This is a competently run business at a fair-to-full price whose one big catalyst is in the rear-view mirror; size it as a quality-cyclical hold-or-pass, not as an asymmetric special-situation.

interrogator_dossier:
# Aptiv PLC (APTV) — Interrogator Forensic Dossier

*Transcripts: Q1 2025 (2025-05-01) through Q1 2026 (2026-05-05), six consecutive quarters spanning the run-up to and completion of the Electrical Distribution Systems (EDS) spin-off into Versigent (VGNT, NYSE), distributed April 1, 2026.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story over these six quarters is a deliberate, well-telegraphed **conglomerate-to-pure-play transformation** executed against a deteriorating auto-production backdrop. It moved through three phases:

**Phase 1 — "Resilient operator weathering the storm" (Q1–Q2 2025).** The opening narrative was defensive and macro-dominated: tariffs, USMCA compliance, China EV-customer mix, FX (the Mexican peso). Kevin Clark led every call with the "in-region, for-region" supply-chain digital twin as the moat. Revenue was flat-to-down ("Revenue totaled $4.8 billion, down 1%," Q1-25), and the entire pitch was *operating execution offsetting external headwinds* — SG&A down 10% in 2024, targeted another 5% in 2025, "more aggressive in terms of looking for opportunities to further reduce costs" (Q1-25). The EDS separation was mentioned as "on track" but was background.

**Phase 2 — "The spin becomes the story" (Q3 2025–Q4 2025).** As 2025 progressed, the separation moved to center stage. By Q4-25 (2026-02-02) management had named the entity ("Versagen," CEO Joe Liatine), set the date ("spin out... on April 1"), and quantified the stranded-cost drag ($70M called out at Investor Day, "$50 million impact in the first full year on a pro forma basis" for NewAptiv plus "~$15 million" public-company setup for the SpinCo). Simultaneously the segment architecture was relabeled: "Advanced Safety and User Experience" became **"Intelligent Systems"** (Q4-25), reframing the legacy ADAS/infotainment business as a software-and-edge-compute growth engine.

**Phase 3 — "New Aptiv: intelligence at the edge" (Q1 2026).** Post-separation (completed April 1, 2026), Clark's prepared remarks were "focused exclusively on New Aptiv." The narrative pivoted hard from *auto-supplier-managing-decline* to *diversified software/edge-compute platform*: "sense, think, act and continually optimize," ~25% non-automotive revenue, Wind River ($6B TAM), and a brand-new **robotics/drones** growth vector (partnerships with Robust AI, Vecna Robotics, Comau). The center of gravity shifted from cost-out and tariffs to TAM expansion and bookings ($7B total awards Q1-26; $20B+ NewAptiv 2026 bookings target).

**Growth drivers introduced:** robotics/cobots/AMRs and drones (new in Q4-25/Q1-26); Wind River edge-AI ecosystem (steadily amplified, "over 20%" growth); non-auto end markets (A&D, telecom, data centers, industrial — "rapidly approaching almost $4 billion" by Q3-25). **Drivers quietly de-emphasized:** the original tariff/USMCA crisis framing (Q1-Q2 2025's dominant topic) faded once it proved "de minimis"; the China-local-OEM-mix-headwind, hammered for four straight quarters, was reframed in Q1-26 as turning "positive growth... in Q2."

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

The headline tension: management claimed **"record" results every single quarter** (record Q-revenue, OI, EPS) while the actual fiscal-year financials show a company whose net income collapsed from $2.94B (2023) to $165M (2025) and EPS from $10.39 to $0.75. The reconciliation is that management speaks almost exclusively in **adjusted, ex-amortization, ex-restructuring, ex-special-items, ex-FX/commodity** terms — and the gap between that scorecard and GAAP reality is the central forensic story.

| Management claim | Quarter | Hard financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "record... earnings per share of $2.17" / "$1.86" / "record EPS" | Q3-25, Q4-25 | FY2025 GAAP EPS **$0.75**, down **-89.2% YoY**; 5-yr EPS CAGR **-21.1%** | **Diverges sharply.** Adjusted "records" are driven heavily by an 18–20% share-count reduction (the $3B ASR completed Apr-1-2025), not earnings growth. The denominator, not the numerator, makes the records. |
| "strong operating performance... flow-through on volume" | every Q | FY net margin **0.8%**; ROIC **6.8%** (below WACC); Gross margin 19.1% (expanding) | **Partial.** Gross-margin expansion and the SG&A program are real and corroborated. But net margin near zero and sub-cost-of-capital ROIC say operating strength is being consumed below the line (amortization, interest on $6.2B net debt, restructuring). |
| "$818M / $584M / $510M operating cash flow... accelerate deleveraging" | Q2–Q4-25 | TTM FCF **$1.09B** (down -5.4% YoY but 3-yr CAGR +54%); P/FCF 9.8x; Net debt **$6.18B** | **Largely supported.** Cash generation is genuine and the deleveraging claim is credible. This is the strongest pillar — FCF is the real asset here, not accounting EPS. |
| "$31 billion in new business awards this year" | Q1–Q3-25 (reaffirmed) | Delivered **$27B** (Q4-25: "short of our target of $31 billion") | **Missed.** A clean, dated guidance miss — the first hard credibility dent. Management blamed timing ("customer awards shifting to 2026"), which is plausible but is exactly the deflection analysts flagged all year. |
| "confident in reaching... over $31 billion" → silently lowered | Q1-26 | NewAptiv 2026 bookings guide **"$20 billion"** (not comparable post-spin) | **Reset, not missed.** The bar moved with the perimeter; reasonable, but the serial "timing shift" language is now a pattern. |
| Margin "expansion of 30 basis points, excluding FX and commodities" | Q1-26 | Margin **-1.65σ vs historical peak**; Net margin 0.8% | **The ex-FX qualifier is load-bearing.** On a reported basis margins compressed (Q4-25 Intelligent Systems margins were "surprisingly weak," -170bps FX hit). The "ex-FX/commodity" framing is honest as a same-store metric but flatters a business taking real FX losses. |

**Net read:** the cash story (FCF, deleveraging) **matches** the numbers; the earnings story is **richer than the trajectory justifies** because it leans on adjustments and a shrinking share count to manufacture "records" atop a -89% GAAP EPS collapse.

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: STABLE-to-mildly-defensive, with a confident pivot in the final quarter.** Management's confidence is remarkably consistent — almost scripted — but the *content* of that confidence shifted from offense to defense and back:

- **Q1-25:** Cautious-defensive. "we're currently in a period of uncertainty due to rapid changes in global trade policies"; explicitly **declined to guide** H2 ("we don't have the visibility to forecast revenue for Q3 and Q4"). Confident on cost control, evasive on demand.
- **Q2-25:** Steady. "record second quarter results," but flagged "ongoing roll-off of legacy user experience programs" and "slowdown... on select Zeekr and NIO programs in China... headwinds for the next few quarters" — *proactively naming* future weakness (a green-flag honesty signal).
- **Q3-25:** Most confident of the year offensively. "$8.4 billion of new business awards," raised full-year guidance — but immediately hedged on Nexperia and trade ("we just overlaid some incremental conservatism").
- **Q4-25:** Mixed. Confident on the spin and bookings, but had to own the $31B miss and explain "surprisingly weak" Intelligent Systems margins (Colin Langan's words, conceded by Clark). Defensive on FX/peso.
- **Q1-26:** Re-energized, offensive. Full "intelligence at the edge" investment-thesis framing, robotics TAM, "$20 billion of bookings... high level of confidence." The tone reset to growth/investment language precisely as the perimeter narrowed to the higher-margin RemainCo.

**Specificity over time:** Management got *more* specific on bookings and segment detail but persistently *less* specific (deliberately) on forward revenue, repeatedly refusing full-year/half-year guidance in 2025 ("we're not providing guidance for the second half"). The shift from "offensive" (TAM, robotics, edge AI) to "defensive" (SG&A -5%, tariff mitigation, FX hedges) and back maps cleanly to the macro cycle and the spin timeline.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM-HIGH on the controllables, MEDIUM on the externals.**

- **Earnings-beat rate: 7/7 (100%)** per the screener, corroborated by the Q1-26 print ($1.71 vs $1.62 consensus). This is a genuine, durable sandbagging-and-deliver pattern on EPS — management consistently sets a beatable bar and clears it, helped by the buyback tailwind.
- **The one hard miss:** the **$31B bookings target → $27B actual** (Q4-25). They reaffirmed "$31 billion" in Q1, Q2, and Q3 ("we continue to expect roughly $31 billion") before conceding the shortfall. The miss was ~13%, blamed entirely on timing. Credibility cost: real but limited, because bookings are a softer KPI than EPS and the "shifted to 2026" explanation is at least internally consistent (2026 guide is $20B+ for the smaller NewAptiv).
- **Cost/cash guidance: reliably delivered.** The SG&A reduction program (-10% 2024, -5% 2025) and the deleveraging/buyback cadence were stated and executed (ASR completed on schedule, 20% of shares retired over 12 months).
- **Pattern:** Sandbag EPS, deliver; over-promise bookings, under-deliver once; execute cost/capital plans precisely. Net: **High** on EPS and capital allocation, **Medium** on commercial momentum (bookings).

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **China mix / growth-over-market (every quarter, the dominant recurring pressure).** Colin Langan (Wells Fargo) pressed it in Q1, Q2, and again on Versagen EV exposure in Q4. Management's answers were consistently directional and non-quantified — "I'm not sure we'll give you an exact dollar amount on the headwind" (Q3-25). The Zeekr/NIO program cancellations (named Q2-25) became the bad news that the earlier "we're on track to match production mix" optimism had soft-pedaled. **This is the clearest deflect-then-deliver-bad-news pattern.**
- **Second-half visibility (2025).** Langan in Q1-25 walked management into the implied H2 acceleration ("a pretty major step up in growth over market") and Clark simply refused: "we're not providing guidance for the second half." Analysts wanted a forecast; management gave a framework.
- **In-source vs outsource ADAS (Q2-25, Tom Narayan/RBC).** A strategic-risk probe — do large OEMs pull ADAS in-house? Management answered confidently ("that trend has reversed course"), which is a *claim about competitors' behavior* that bears watching.
- **Versagen stranded costs and pro-forma EBITDA bridge (Q4-25, James Picariello).** Sharp, technical pressure on the $75M EBITDA gap, stranded costs ($70M), and standup costs — management answered cleanly, a green flag.
- **Intelligent Systems margin weakness (Q4-25, Langan).** "surprisingly weak" — management conceded three drivers (FX -170bps, engineering-credit timing, robotics investment). Honest, but it confirmed the segment's margins are lumpy and FX-exposed.

**What analysts worry about that management isn't fully addressing:** whether the bookings-to-revenue conversion is real (timing slips two years running), and whether non-auto/robotics is a genuine margin mix-shifter or an investment sink ("incremental investment... will continue into 2026," Q4-25).

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **GAAP earnings collapse masked by "record" adjusted EPS.** FY2025 GAAP EPS $0.75 (-89%) vs a steady drumbeat of "record" adjusted quarters — the gap is bridged by adjustments + an 18–20% share-count reduction. The "records" are partly financial engineering.
🔴 **Serial bookings "timing shifts."** The $31B → $27B miss, blamed on slippage to 2026, then a 2026 guide that itself notes awards "could shift." A two-year pattern of moving the goalposts forward.
🔴 **Insider conviction is negative.** Open-market reality: **2 buys (9,800 sh) vs 15 sells (113,488 sh)** — the grant-inflated buy-ratio in the scan is misleading; the real signal is net selling. No insider is backing up the truck on the "New Aptiv" re-rate story.
🔴 **China growth-over-market chronically negative**, with program cancellations (Zeekr/NIO) that the prior optimism had not flagged; "down 14%" in NewAptiv China Q1-26.
🔴 **FX/peso is a structural, recurring OI drag** management cannot fully hedge (peso from ~21 to sub-18; "a ton of OI impact"), and Versagen carries the un-hedged operational exposure.

🟢 **Free-cash-flow generation is genuine and improving** ($818M OCF in Q4-25; TTM FCF $1.09B; 3-yr FCF CAGR +54%), funding both buybacks and deleveraging — the real asset.
🟢 **The spin was executed on time and as promised** (Versigent/VGNT, April 1, 2026) — management did exactly what it said over four quarters, a credibility deposit.
🟢 **Proactive bad-news disclosure** (named Zeekr/NIO roll-offs and legacy-program decline *before* they fully hit) and clean answers to technical stranded-cost questions.
🟢 **100% EPS beat rate (7/7)** and a Piotroski 8/9 — operationally the business is well-run and financially clean despite the headwinds.
🟢 **Real cost discipline** (SG&A -10% then -5%, seven facilities consolidated) — the controllables are managed tightly.

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **The segment rename is the tell.** "Advanced Safety and User Experience" → **"Intelligent Systems"** (Q4-25). This is not cosmetic; it reframes a business carrying a *declining* legacy infotainment/UX roll-off as a forward-looking edge-compute platform, conveniently as the spin approached and the equity story needed a growth label.

The transcript's "Versigent" closing-day reference matches the filed entity. (Cosmetic, but worth noting the transcript transcription is imperfect.)

- **Competition framing softened on in-sourcing risk** ("OEMs... less desire to do things internally," Q2-25) precisely when that risk was most topical — a confident assertion that, if wrong, undercuts the entire ADAS bull case.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING (structurally complete) → STABLE post-spin.**

Aptiv has just finished a clean, well-executed conglomerate-to-pure-play pivot: the low-margin, copper-indexed, peso-exposed EDS commodity-wiring business was spun out as Versigent (VGNT) on **April 1, 2026**, leaving a higher-margin "New Aptiv" of Intelligent Systems + Engineered Components with ~25% non-auto revenue and a nascent robotics/edge-AI optionality layer. The segment-revenue block in the screener still includes EDS (41% of revenue) and is therefore **stale** for any go-forward sum-of-parts — value only the two remaining segments. Per peer comps, APTV screens **rich, not cheap**, vs Lear/Magna/BorgWarner (6–8x P/FCF) despite the optically low 9.8x raw multiple. This is a competently run business at a fair-to-full price whose one big catalyst is in the rear-view mirror; size it as a quality-cyclical hold-or-pass, not as an asymmetric special-situation.

interrogator_dossier:
# Aptiv PLC (APTV) — Forensic Dossier

*Interrogator: Claude Opus 4.8 · capital-allocation committee · transcripts Q1'25 → Q1'26 (6 calls) cross-referenced against the screener financials.*

**Decisive structural fact up front:** the load-bearing event in this story — the spin-off of Electrical Distribution Systems (EDS) into **Versigent (NYSE: VGNT)** — **completed on April 1, 2026**, with a 1-for-3 distribution to holders of record March 17, 2026. The catalyst that every "sum-of-parts discount" thesis on Aptiv leaned on for three years has **already fired**. The screener's SEGMENT REVENUE block (EDS 41% / ECG 31% / ASUX 27%) is a *pre-spin* snapshot and is now stale: EDS is no longer inside APTV — original holders already hold it as a separate stock worth ~$45/sh. Any SoP that still credits APTV with the EDS segment double-counts value that has left the building.

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The arc across these six calls is the controlled demolition of a conglomerate into a "tech-stack" pure-play, narrated with unusual consistency.

- **Q1'25 (May-2025):** The story is *resilience under tariff shock + the EDS separation "on track."* Kevin Clark leads with "exceeding our guidance range," a completed "$3 billion accelerated share repurchase" that "reduced our share count by 18%," and the framing that "the future is electrified, software-defined and connected." Tariffs dominate the Q&A — the "$5 billion of product annually from Mexico, of which over 99% is USMCA compliant" defense is established here and never wavers.
- **Q2'25:** Same spine, with the EDS spin given a date ("Investor Day in November") and the *moat* language introduced — "sensor-to-cloud," "further strengthening our competitive moat." First admission of demand cracks: "a recent slowdown in production schedules on select Zeekr and NIO programs in China… headwinds for the next few quarters."
- **Q3'25:** Bookings inflect ("$8.4 billion of new business awards"), spin date sharpens to "by the end of the first quarter of 2026," and the EDS-name "Versagen" appears. China under-mix and two cancelled local-OEM programs are now openly carried as headwinds.
- **Q4'25 (Feb-2026):** The pivot is consummated in language: segments are *renamed* — Advanced Safety & User Experience → **Intelligent Systems**, Engineered Components Group → **Engineered Components** — and EDS-CEO Joe Liatine appears as a separate voice. Full-year bookings landed at **$27B vs the $31B target** ("short of our target… a result of customer awards shifting to 2026"). The robotics/Wind River/non-auto narrative is elevated to load-bearing.
- **Q1'26 (May-2026):** Spin **complete**. Clark's prepared remarks are "focused exclusively on New Aptiv." The story is fully re-based to "intelligence at the edge… sense, think, act and continually optimize," "roughly 1/4 of our business is in markets outside of automotive," robotics/drones/A&D as the growth engine, and a 2026 bookings target cut to "more than $20 billion" for New Aptiv (vs $30B+ for total Aptiv incl. Versigent).

Growth drivers *introduced*: Wind River edge-AI, robotics (Robust AI, Vecna, Comau), drones/A&D, India. Drivers *quietly de-emphasized*: the original EV-content-per-vehicle supercycle (EV growth guided to a modest "roughly 15%," mostly China hybrids) and the China revenue-mix catch-up (repeatedly slipped, now reframed as "be with the winners," not match production mix).

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Transcript evidence | Hard financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Record" revenue / OI / EPS "every quarter" | Q3'25: "record financial results, including revenue, operating income and earnings per share" | FY revenue 19.71B→**20.40B** (+3.5%); these are *adjusted, ex-special-item, continuing-ops* records | **Half-true.** Operationally real on the adjusted line; the GAAP picture is far uglier (below). |
| Strong operating execution / margin expansion | Repeatedly: "margin expansion of 30 basis points, excluding FX and commodities" (Q1'26) | Gross margin 19.1% (expanding); **net margin 0.8%**; FCF margin 7.5% | **Diverges hard at the bottom line.** Adjusted OI grows; GAAP net income **collapsed from 1.79B (FY24) to 165M (FY25)** and EPS from 6.96 → **0.75 (-89% YoY)**. The "record EPS" is the adjusted, buyback-flattered number; the reported franchise barely cleared breakeven. |
| Aggressive buyback / shareholder return | Q1'25: "$3B ASR… share count reduced 18%… 20% retired over 12 months" | Diluted shares: weighted avg **220.75M (Q4'25) → 213.8M (Q1'26)**; bvps_5y +7.9% | **TRUE and the single most credible claim.** This is real, executed financial engineering — the share count is down materially and is the mechanical driver of "record EPS" on flat-ish OI. |
| Deleveraging on track | Q1'25 "accelerate our deleveraging"; Q1'26 redeemed "$1,847 million of senior notes" with Versigent proceeds | Net debt 6.18B (total Aptiv, Mar-2026); post-spin New Aptiv net debt ≈ **~$4.3B** after the $1.85B redemption | **TRUE.** The spin was used to delever, exactly as promised. Altman-Z 2.1 (grey zone) is total-Aptiv pre-deleverage. |
| Tariffs "de minimis," fully passable | Q1'25→Q1'26 consistent: "99% USMCA compliant… any remaining… passed on to customers"; "collected 95–100% of what we pursued" | Margins held flat-to-up through the tariff year | **Credible and corroborated.** The pass-through track record is real; this is not where the bodies are. |
| Demand "not deteriorating" | Q1'26: "We're not deteriorating. Maybe that's a better way to describe it." | Revenue +1% Q1'26 "despite a deterioration in underlying vehicle production"; China New Aptiv **-14%** | **Thin.** Growth is now coming from *bookings narrative and mix*, not volume. The honest read is flat-to-declining underlying auto demand offset by cost-out and buybacks. |
| $31B bookings target | Held all year Q1–Q3'25 | Landed **$27B** (Q4'25) — a 13% miss | **MISS, openly disclosed.** Reframed as "timing… shifting to 2026," which is partly real but also the first clean guidance miss in the set. |

**Net:** The adjusted/operational story is genuinely competent — cost-out (SG&A -10% in '24, another -5% in '25), pass-throughs, and buybacks are all real and verified. But the *reported* earnings power cratered 89% in FY25, and the screener fair-value models scream over-valuation (Buffett FV 21.84, EPV MoS **-79%**, IV15 -100%, MoS -67%) against a stock that — even post-spin — trades at $62.89 vs a DCF FV of 56.3. The narrative is **richer than the GAAP trajectory justifies** but **poorer than the buyback-flattered adjusted EPS implies**.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: STABLE-to-mildly-defensive.** Clark and Laroyia are among the more disciplined, non-promotional management teams in the supplier group — confident but specific, and they self-flag bad news (Zeekr/NIO cancellations, the $27B booking miss, Q4 Intelligent Systems margin weakness) rather than burying it.

- **Q1'25 — offensive/confident:** "started the year strong," "first quarter record," "long-term growth drivers… remain fully intact."
- **Q2–Q3'25 — confident but hedging on the back half:** "we don't have the visibility to forecast revenue for Q3 and Q4"; "it's impossible to give you a specific answer" (on Nexperia conservatism).
- **Q4'25–Q1'26 — defensive-pragmatic:** language shifts unmistakably from growth to *efficiency and resilience* — "relentless focus on our cost structure," "resilient operating model," "manage through internally." The single most telling tonal tell is Q1'26's "We're not deteriorating. Maybe that's a better way to describe it" — a CEO reaching for the least-bad framing of flat demand.

Guidance specificity *decreased* through 2025 (repeated refusal to guide H2) and only firmed once the spin clarified the entity. The shift from offensive (content-per-vehicle, EV supercycle) to defensive (cost-out, mix, supply-chain-as-moat) is real and consistent with a maturing, cyclically-pressured auto-supplier base.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM-HIGH.** The financials carry a **100% earnings beat rate (7/7 quarters)** — strong on its face, but for a company whose adjusted EPS is propelled by a known buyback, beating the adjusted line is a lower bar than it looks. Where management gave a *hard, falsifiable* target — the **$31B bookings number** — they **missed by 13%** ($27B). They disclosed it cleanly and re-based 2026 lower ($20B New Aptiv / $30B+ total), which is honest, not sandbagging. The deleveraging and buyback commitments were delivered precisely. The pattern is: **operationally reliable on the controllables (cost, capital allocation, pass-throughs), unreliable on demand-dependent top-line targets** — which is exactly what you'd expect from a competent management running a business whose end-market they don't control.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

The analyst corps (Wells Fargo/Langan, Goldman/Delaney, RBC/Narayan, Deutsche/Yu, TD Cowen/Michaeli, Wolfe/Rosner) circled the same three sore points all year:

1. **China revenue-mix gap.** Langan, every call: "your mix in China… 10% under market… when does that start to normalize?" Management's answer drifted from "at the end of this year" (Q2'25) to "be with the winners… we'll never perfectly match" (Q4'25) to confessing New Aptiv China **-14%** in Q1'26. This is the question they most consistently *re-framed rather than answered*, and it kept turning into worse news (the Zeekr/NIO/top-OEM cancellations).
2. **Back-half visibility / the "growth-over-market" bridge.** Langan (Q1'25): "does that imply a pretty major step up in growth in the second half?" — never answered ("we're not providing guidance for the second half").
3. **SVA / software-defined-vehicle monetization.** The bookings-to-revenue gap on Smart Vehicle Architecture: $5B+ "opportunities" but only "$150M–$200M" of 2025 revenue. Management is candid that "software-defined vehicle" timelines pushed out, especially in NA/Europe.

The thing analysts are *not* yet pressing hard, and management is *not* volunteering: what New Aptiv's *organic auto* growth actually is once you strip mix, buybacks, and the non-auto growth that's still only ~25% of the base.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **GAAP earnings collapse:** EPS 6.96 → 0.75 (-89% YoY), net income 1.79B → 165M. Even if largely amortization/special-items/impairment-driven, a sub-1% net margin is a real solvency-adjacent fact (Altman-Z 2.1, grey zone).
🔴 **$31B bookings miss to $27B** — the first clean miss of a publicly-held hard target.
🔴 **China structurally under-mixing** and disproportionately exposed to the top local OEM's production cuts (Q1'26 ECG hit).
🔴 **Insider conviction is negative** on the real (open-market) signal: **2 buys (9.8k sh) vs 16 sells (116k sh)** — the screener's 0.08 grant-inflated "buy ratio" masks net selling.
🔴 **Catalyst already spent:** the spin — the entire special-sit reason-to-own — is *complete*. There is no remaining "stub-discount" arbitrage; the parts have been separated and are independently priced.

🟢 **Capital allocation is genuinely shareholder-friendly and executed:** ~20% of shares retired in 12 months, $1.85B debt redeemed with spin proceeds.
🟢 **Cost discipline is real and repeated:** SG&A -10% ('24), -5% ('25), with further cuts in flight.
🟢 **Tariff/pass-through management is best-in-class** (99% USMCA, 95–100% recovery rate).
🟢 **Non-auto / Wind River / robotics is a real, higher-margin, faster-growing ~25% of the business** — "high single-digit/double-digit" growth, "$6B TAM" Wind River, A&D + data-center + robotics design wins. This is the one credible *terminal-value-additive* leg.
🟢 **Bookings re-accelerated** ($8.4B Q3'25; $7B total / $4.6B New Aptiv Q1'26) — demand for the portfolio is intact even as volume is soft.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **The rename is the tell.** "Advanced Safety and User Experience" → "Intelligent Systems" and "Engineered Components Group" → "Engineered Components" is not cosmetic — it's management repositioning the equity away from a cyclical *auto-parts* multiple toward a *software/edge-compute* multiple ahead of the spin. Watch whether the market grants it.
- **"Moat" entered the lexicon in Q2'25** ("strengthening our competitive moat," "supply-chain digital twin," "in-region for-region") and recurs every call thereafter — a deliberate effort to argue switching-cost/scale durability as content-per-vehicle growth slows.
- **Robotics investment is being front-loaded into margins now** (Q4'25/Q1'26: "accelerated the investment… that will continue into 2026"), which is *why* Intelligent Systems margins were "surprisingly weak" in Q4 — a deliberate trade of near-term margin for a non-auto TAM option. Honest, but it caps near-term earnings.
- **New CFO-led discipline holds:** Laroyia's peso-hedge candor (Q4'25: "hedged about 95% below 18") and stranded-cost transparency ($70M, $50M New Aptiv / $15M Versigent stand-up) signal a finance org that tells you the math rather than the spin.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING (toward STABLE).** This is a competently-run, shareholder-aligned tier-1 supplier executing a clean conglomerate-breakup into a focused "intelligence-at-the-edge" pure-play, financed with real deleveraging and aggressive buybacks. It is *not* a deteriorating or distressed name; nor is it a hidden compounder — GAAP earnings power is depressed and the top line is volume-flat.

**The single most important thing to weigh before sizing: the catalyst is gone.** The EDS spin — the only hard, dated, special-situation event in this story — **completed April 1, 2026**. Versigent (VGNT) is already a separate $3.2B-cap public stock the original holders received. There is no remaining sum-of-parts arbitrage inside APTV; "the parts" have been physically separated and are now independently priced by the market. What remains is a *valuation* question on the New Aptiv stub (Intelligent Systems + Engineered Components, ~$13B revenue), not an event question. At $62.89 the stub trades roughly in line with — to slightly rich versus — its true auto-supplier peers (VC ~6–7x FCF; the discount to a software multiple is a *bet the market re-rates it*, not a realized gap). Size it as a fairly-valued, post-catalyst cyclical with a real but minority non-auto optionality leg — **not** as an asymmetric special-sit.

**ECONOMIC MOAT.** Type: **switching-costs + scale-cost** (designed-in content on locked, multi-year vehicle platforms; in-region/for-region manufacturing footprint and supply-chain "digital twin"; Wind River intangible/embedded-software lock-in). Width: **NARROW**, and on the returns evidence **ERODING** — ROIC **6.8%** sits *below* a reasonable WACC (~9–10%), ROE 12.3% is buyback-levered, gross margin (~19%) is mid-pack and not expanding through the bottom line, and reported net margin has cratered to 0.8%. A moat that produces below-WACC returns is, by definition, not a wide one. The Wind River/non-auto leg is the only genuinely widening sub-moat.

**SECULAR THREAT.** **Manageable-to-material, NOT terminal.** The relevant structural force is **autonomous-mobility / drivetrain electrification** — the long-horizon reshaping of the vehicle. Crucially, Aptiv is largely on the *right* side of it: ADAS, active safety, high-voltage electrical content, zonal/SVA architecture and software are content-*adders* as cars electrify and add autonomy, not content-losers (unlike pure ICE-drivetrain suppliers). The genuine threats are (a) OEM in-sourcing of ADAS/software (management argues this trend has "reversed course," with evidence) and (b) China local-OEM price/share dynamics. This is a cyclical-and-competitive story with a structurally-*supported* terminal base, not a melting one — the cheapness signals (EPV -79%, Buffett FV ~22) are a **depressed-earnings fear-print on a low-quality-but-not-shrinking franchise**, partly justified by genuinely poor current returns, not a clean mispricing.

bull_thesis: The bull case is that the market re-rates a simpler, less indebted "New Aptiv" higher now that the spin-off is done. On April 1, 2026 Aptiv completed the separation of its lowest-margin, copper- and peso-exposed Electrical Distribution Systems segment into a new company called Versigent (VGNT). In the process Aptiv received a $2,125M cash distribution and used about $2.1B to repay debt, bringing New Aptiv to 2.3x gross and 1.9x net leverage (debt relative to annual earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization) from pre-spin net debt of roughly $6.2B. What remains are the two higher-margin, faster-shifting segments: Intelligent Systems (driver-assistance technology, Wind River software, edge computing) and Engineered Components (high-speed interconnect and high-voltage parts). The structural tailwinds are real and are backed up by the earnings calls. Non-automotive revenue is in the high $3 billion range, rapidly approaching almost $4 billion and growing high single digits, almost double digits (third quarter of 2025), across aerospace and defense, telecom, data center, and a new robotics push (partnerships with Robust AI, Vecna, and Comau; on the first-quarter 2026 call Wind River cited a roughly $6B addressable market and a $4,000-to-$5,000 content-per-unit comparison). Software and services grow at double-digit rates, with Wind River up more than 20% for several quarters running, and China local-automaker bookings reached 85% with China local automakers, about $5B of awards in 2025 with an export-platform focus. Operating execution is the most credible part of the story: gross margin is expanding, selling and administrative costs were cut about 10% in 2024 and another 5% in 2025, the company beat earnings expectations in all 7 of the last 7 quarters, and a disciplined buyback retired roughly 20% of shares in a year, creating genuine per-share gains. Clean trailing-twelve-month diluted earnings are $1.68 per share (FMP data, March 2026) and first-quarter 2026 adjusted earnings were $1.71 per share for the whole company. At $63.68 and about 9 times free cash flow, the stock prices in commodity-supplier economics for a business management is steering toward an edge-intelligence software platform (the segment was literally renamed "Intelligent Systems"). If margins and return on invested capital (the profit a company earns on the money put into the business) inflect even modestly toward the best tier-1 suppliers as the lower-margin segment falls away and the software mix rises, a 12-to-14 times earnings multiple on $4.50 to $5.50 of New Aptiv earnings per share supports $60 to $75 or more, with the cleaned-up balance sheet and the buyback as a cushion if things go wrong. A discounted-cash-flow estimate (which capitalizes research and development) puts fair value at $56.3 and the average analyst target is $88.63, about 39% above today's price, bracketing a constructive base; owner-earnings and intrinsic-value models point to a 40% and 31% margin of safety respectively, suggesting that on an owner-earnings basis the remaining business is cheap.
bear_thesis: The bear case is that the deep-value opportunity here — valuing the company's pieces separately and waiting for a spin-off to surface a hidden discount — has already played out, and what is left is a low-return, low-growth tier-1 auto supplier dressed up as a tech company. The spin-off completed April 1, 2026; the $2.1B in cash has been received and the debt repaid, so there is no remaining hidden value gap to harvest. Any model still crediting the Electrical Distribution Systems business (41% of revenue and an $8.82B segment in the source data) as upside is double-counting an event that already happened, since that business is now Versigent and no longer part of Aptiv. Strip away the spin-off and the fundamentals are pedestrian: return on invested capital is 6.8% (below the company's cost of capital and below peers), net margin is 0.8%, revenue growth is an anemic 3.5% (a 5.3% average over three years), and reported net income collapsed from $2.94B in 2023 to $1.79B in 2024 to $165M in 2025. The much-touted "record earnings per share" is manufactured by buybacks on a flat, low-return base, and earnings per share have actually shrunk at a 21.1% annual rate over five years. China — supposedly the growth engine — has been a chronic drag: roughly 10% below market growth for years, program cancellations (Zeekr, NIO), and New Aptiv China revenue down 14% in the first quarter of 2026, with normalization promised "in Q2" yet again. The growth segment carries its own margin headwind: Intelligent Systems margins were described as surprisingly weak in the fourth quarter of 2025 (a 170-basis-point currency hit plus stepped-up robotics investment that will continue into 2026). The $31B bookings target was missed (with $27B actually delivered) and re-based to more than $20B. Insiders are net sellers in the open market (2 buys totaling 9,800 shares against 16 sells totaling 116,488 shares) and analysts have been cutting their price targets, down about 15%. Robotics is currently a cost line, not a revenue line. The headline 62% shortfall against a Buffett-style fair value of $24.4 flags that the cheap multiple sits on a structurally low-return base, exposed to cyclical auto production, currency (the peso), and commodity (copper and resin) costs. A re-rating to a tech-style multiple requires a margin and return inflection that has not shown up in any number yet.
consensus_delta: Where we disagree with the market. For a name that screens as deep value, the usual test is whether the market is pricing terminal decline into a company that actually has stable or growing earnings power. Here the problem is the opposite: the market — an average analyst target of $88.63 (39% above the price) and a discounted-cash-flow estimate of $56.3 — is pricing in a post-spin re-rating that the numbers have not earned. The screener's headline 62% shortfall against a Buffett-style fair value of $24.4 is itself distorted, because it keys off 2025 reported net income of $165M (which is loaded with amortization, restructuring, and spin-off noise) rather than the roughly $1.68 trailing-twelve-month or roughly $5.00 New Aptiv run-rate earnings. The genuinely mistaken market assumption is the bullish one — that Intelligent Systems deserves a tech multiple — when return on invested capital is 6.8%, net margin is 0.8%, and the growth segment's own margins were surprisingly weak in the fourth quarter of 2025 under ongoing robotics investment. There is no large hidden value gap left: the Electrical Distribution Systems business was the gap, and it has already been distributed to shareholders (1 Versigent share for every 3 Aptiv shares) with the cash used to cut debt. So our disagreement with the market is small and, if anything, runs the other way — the 39% upside in the consensus target embeds optimism the fundamentals have not validated, while the deep-value catalyst is spent.
forcing_function: The catalyst — the event that was supposed to force a re-rating — was the Electrical Distribution Systems spin-off, and it has already happened (record date March 17, 2026; distribution April 1, 2026; the $2,125M cash distribution received; about $2.1B of gross debt repaid; New Aptiv now at 1.9x net leverage). That catalyst is spent. The only events left are ordinary fundamental reports: second-quarter 2026 earnings (around early August, 39 days out) where the promised turn to positive growth in China and a margin recovery must finally show up, and the broader case that New Aptiv deserves a higher multiple, which needs management to convert its bookings (a target of more than $20B for 2026) into accelerating revenue toward the 4% to 7% out-year range. There is no fixed-date, binding, asymmetric corporate event ahead — only the burden of proof on management's promise of a transformed "New Aptiv."
valley_of_death: The risky window (the next 3 to 9 months) holds several drags, all of them ordinary rather than existential. First, China is still negative: New Aptiv China was down 14% in the first quarter of 2026, with positive growth merely promised "in Q2" after years of the same promise. Second, currency and commodity headwinds: the peso swung from about 20.75 to below 18 (a large hit to operating income), and copper and resin inflation is passed through on roughly a three-month lag, so margin recovery is delayed. Third, stepped-up robotics and engineering investment that management explicitly said will continue into 2026, a self-inflicted margin headwind on the growth segment. Fourth, leftover costs of the spin-off (about $70M in stranded costs, about $50M in the first year for the remaining company, and $15M in public-company costs). Fifth, cyclical auto-production weakness (management cited a deterioration in underlying vehicle production in the first quarter of 2026), plus Nexperia chip-supply and Middle East supply-chain risk. There is no looming debt maturity (the company just de-levered to 1.9x net) and no cash-burn hump (trailing-twelve-month free cash flow is $1.09B) — so the risk is an earnings disappointment and multiple compression, not insolvency.
positioning_washout: There is limited forced-selling pressure to exploit here. The spin-off already drove the largest mechanical event — index funds and other holders rebalancing as Versigent was distributed (1 share for every 3) and Aptiv's index weight reset; that flush is largely behind us. Aptiv is not an income stock, so there is no dividend-focused investor base that a payout cut could force out. Insiders are already net sellers in the open market (2 buys totaling 9,800 shares against 16 sells totaling 116,488 shares) and analyst price targets are down about 15%, so the de-rating is already in progress rather than waiting on a single moment of capitulation. There is no concentrated forced-seller setup left to take advantage of.
moderator_conclusion: Our call is to pass for now, with low conviction. The thesis that brought Aptiv into a deep-value screen — a discount between the company's market value and the separate worth of its parts, to be unlocked by spinning out Electrical Distribution Systems — has already played out. The spin-off completed April 1, 2026; Versigent (VGNT) now trades on its own; Aptiv took the $2,125M cash distribution and repaid about $2.1B of gross debt to reach 1.9x net leverage. The source file's segment block (Electrical Distribution Systems at 41% of revenue) and net-debt figure ($6.18B) are stale, pre-spin numbers; valuing that business as upside double-counts an event that already happened. Rebuilding the sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) on the true remaining company — Intelligent Systems plus Engineered Components, with the spun-off segment excluded — lands a base case of about $58 a share, essentially at the $63.68 price, with a favorable case around $75 and an adverse case around $42. The discount that justified entering this as a special situation is gone, and the residual reward-versus-risk (roughly 18% up against 34% down) is unfavorable. Under the current market regime, the approach rewards fixed-date, in-window, structural special situations in thinly covered corners of the market and deliberately penalizes catalysts that are already past or out of window — a spent spin-off is exactly the case it penalizes, and this is a heavily covered name (full sell-side coverage, a consensus target of $88.63), so the thin-coverage edge is absent. This is now a fundamental bet on a tier-1 auto supplier achieving a margin and return inflection (from 6.8% return on invested capital and a 0.8% net margin) that has not appeared, against persistent China weakness, currency and commodity drag, and ongoing robotics-investment margin headwinds. Separately, judging the value case on its own — setting aside the spent catalyst and the market regime — the remaining business is genuinely middling-to-fair rather than a trap, which we'd score a notch higher: the cleaned-up balance sheet at 1.9x net leverage, the disciplined buyback that retired about 20% of shares, a real shift toward software and non-automotive revenue (Wind River up more than 20%, non-automotive around $4B), and the owner-earnings (40% margin of safety) and intrinsic-value (31% margin of safety) models all say the remaining business is reasonably to modestly cheap on owner earnings, even though the headline 62% shortfall is distorted by reported-earnings noise. The narrow but real moat and the manageable secular threat do not pull that value judgment lower (the moat is not eroding and the secular pressure is not terminal). So the value view sits a little above the catalyst view: fairly-valued to slightly-cheap as a fundamental holding, but the special-situation edge is dead.
sop_breakdown: Base-case sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) for New Aptiv is about $58 a share, with the Electrical Distribution Systems business / Versigent excluded because it was already spun off on April 1, 2026 and should not be counted. A cross-check on earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization (about $3.0B at 7 times) implies $21B of enterprise value, and an earnings cross-check (New Aptiv earnings of about $5.00 a share at 12 times) implies $60 a share. Blending the three approaches — about $44 on sales, about $68 on operating earnings, about $60 on per-share earnings — points to roughly $57 to $60 a share. The base case is about $58 a share, essentially at the $63.68 price — the deep-value gap closed when the spin-off happened.
risk_reward: Roughly even to poor. Upside to fair value (about $58 in the base case, about $75 in the favorable case) from $63.68 ranges from down 8% to up 18%; downside to the bear case (about $42) is down 34%. So the reward-versus-risk is about 18% up against 34% down on the favorable-versus-adverse spread, an unfavorable roughly 0.5-to-1; on a base-versus-bear basis it is roughly down 8% against down 34%. The completed spin-off removed the asymmetry — the discount is largely gone, leaving cyclical, currency, and China downside against a modest software re-rating upside.
peer_comps_note: The verdict against peers is in line. The real peers are Lear (LEA), BorgWarner (BWA), TE Connectivity (TE), and Magna (MGA), plus Forvia (FR.PA and FRVIA.PA) in the broader universe. At about 9 times free cash flow, New Aptiv is cheaper than TE Connectivity (about 18 times) but in line with Magna (about 8 times), with a return on invested capital of 6.8% that sits below peers — so the discount is justified by below-peer returns, not by a hidden, name-specific advantage. The peer group itself de-rated (the auto-cyclical sector moves together); the cheapness is shared sector-wide rather than specific to Aptiv. The one legitimate above-peer lever is the software and Wind River plus non-automotive mix, which could earn a multiple somewhere between a commodity wiring-harness maker (Forvia, about 6 times) and a connectivity specialist (TE Connectivity, about 18 times) — but the margins do not yet justify a premium to Lear or BorgWarner. Anchor the sum-of-the-parts to about 7 times operating earnings and about 12 times per-share earnings (between a trough tier-1 supplier and the tech aspiration), not to TE Connectivity's 18 times.
interrogator_dossier:
# Aptiv PLC (APTV) — A Deep Dive on Management and the Setup

*Transcripts from the first quarter of 2025 through the first quarter of 2026 (6 calls), cross-referenced against the screener financials. A read on capital allocation for the director.*

> **Key context the screener block gets wrong (verified live):** the headline metrics (net debt of $6.18B, and a segment block with Electrical Distribution Systems at $8.82B, or 41% of revenue) are a pre-spin snapshot. On April 1, 2026 Aptiv completed the spin-off of Electrical Distribution Systems into Versigent (NYSE: VGNT) — 1 Versigent share for every 3 Aptiv shares. That business is gone from New Aptiv. Versigent took on $2.1B of new debt and paid Aptiv a $2,125M cash distribution, of which Aptiv used about $2.1B to repay gross debt, taking New Aptiv pro-forma leverage to 2.3x gross and 1.9x net (debt relative to annual earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization). So the "deep-value, 41%-of-revenue" framing is already realized and spent — this catalyst has fired, and the true remaining company is the two higher-margin segments (Intelligent Systems plus Engineered Components), not the three-segment conglomerate the input describes.

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story compresses and resets across six quarters. The first quarter of 2025 opens defensively: management cites a period of uncertainty due to rapid changes in global trade policies, refuses to guide the second half ("we don't have the visibility to forecast revenue for Q3 and Q4"), and leans hard on what it can control — a completed $3B accelerated share repurchase that cut the share count 18% (20% over the trailing year) and an aggressive cost program (selling and administrative costs reduced by over 10% in 2024, with another 5% targeted for 2025). The strategic spine throughout is the Electrical Distribution Systems separation ("on track"), which evolves from a planned event (first quarter of 2025) to a named entity, Versigent, with a leadership team (fourth quarter of 2025) to completed on April 1, 2026 (first quarter of 2026).

The growth narrative shifts decisively from automotive cyclical recovery toward two durable threads: first, non-automotive penetration — "high $3 billion, rapidly approaching almost $4 billion... growing high single digits, almost double digits" (third quarter of 2025), spanning aerospace and defense, telecom, data center, industrial, and a new robotics push (Robust AI, Vecna, and Comau partnerships, late 2025 into the first quarter of 2026); and second, China local-automaker share — bookings reaching 85% with China local automakers, an export-platform focus, and about $5 billion of China awards in 2025. By the first quarter of 2026 the segments are even renamed ("Advanced Safety & User Experience" becomes "Intelligent Systems") and the prepared remarks pivot fully to a clean "New Aptiv" investment story: "sense, think, act and continually optimize," about a quarter of revenue non-automotive, software and services growing double digits.

Quietly dropped: the early-2025 tariff-mitigation drumbeat (USMCA, Mexico flows) fades as it proved minor; the $31B bookings target was missed ($27B actually delivered for 2025, with awards "shifting to 2026") and then re-based downward to more than $20B for New Aptiv in 2026.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | Hard financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Record EPS," repeated, buyback-driven | First through fourth quarter of 2025 | Earnings per share: 2023 $10.39, 2024 $6.96, 2025 $0.75 (down 89% year over year); five-year earnings-per-share trend down 21.1% a year | **Diverges hard.** The "record" adjusted quarterly earnings per share are real on a per-share basis (share count down about 20%), but reported net income cratered to $165M in 2025 on amortization, restructuring, and special items the adjusted figure excludes. The headline minus-89% is spin-off and restructuring noise; trailing-twelve-month diluted earnings of $1.68 (FMP, March 2026) is the cleaner run-rate. |
| Revenue "strength across multiple areas," records | 2025 | Revenue: 2024 $19.71B, 2025 $20.40B (up 3.5%); three-year average growth 5.3% | **Matches, modestly.** Low-single-digit organic growth is real but anemic — the China mix headwind (about 10% below market) and program cancellations (Zeekr, NIO) are cited repeatedly. |
| Margin expansion via cost discipline and performance | All | Gross margin 19.1% (expanding); net margin 0.8%; margins well below the historical peak; return on invested capital 6.8%, return on equity 12.3% | **Partially supported.** Gross margin is genuinely expanding and operating execution is the most credible part of the story, but consolidated margins sit well below history, and a 6.8% return on invested capital is below the cost of capital and below tier-1 peers — operating wins are being eaten by currency (the peso, below 18), commodities (copper and resin), and mix. |
| Deleveraging and capital-allocation discipline | All | Net debt $6.18B pre-spin; free-cash-flow margin 7.5%, trailing-twelve-month free cash flow $1.09B; about 9.2 times free cash flow | **Strongly supported and now realized.** The spin-off delivered a $2.1B cash distribution, about $2.1B of gross debt repaid, New Aptiv at 1.9x net. This is the single most credible, executed claim. |
| "$31B new business bookings" in 2025 | First through third quarter of 2025 | Delivered $27B ("short of target") | **Missed**, then walked down to more than $20B (New Aptiv) for 2026. The bookings narrative is honest about the shortfall (timing, trade), but the target was a stretch. |

**Net read:** the narrative is richer than the reported bottom line (which collapsed) but poorer than the "record EPS" drumbeat implies — the per-share records are buyback-manufactured on a flat-to-low-growth, below-cost-of-capital base. The one place the narrative matches the numbers is deleveraging, which is now done.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY — Rising (off a defensive low)

- **First quarter of 2025 (defensive low):** "period of uncertainty," refuses second-half guidance, "difficult to determine how the changing market dynamics will impact the second half." Confidence channeled into cost cuts and buybacks, not demand.
- **Second quarter of 2025 (steadying):** "record second quarter results," "strengthening our competitive moat," but still flags Zeekr and NIO roll-offs "for the next few quarters."
- **Third quarter of 2025 (offensive):** "another strong quarter," raises full-year guidance, $8.4B in bookings, but injects fresh caution on Nexperia chip supply and "incremental conservatism" into the fourth quarter.
- **Fourth quarter of 2025 and first quarter of 2026 (confident re-launch):** full pivot to the "New Aptiv" investment story, a 4% to 7% growth target, "positive growth in China in Q2," robotics addressable market of "$6 billion." The language moves from macro-defensive to strategically offensive — but it is a promissory offense (bookings, addressable markets, out-year targets) rather than delivered acceleration.

The chief financial officer (Laroyia) is consistently specific on currency (peso 20.75 to below 18, "95% hedged below 18 for 2026"), commodities (copper indexed, roughly three-month pass-through), and stranded costs ($70M, $50M in the first year for the remaining company) — a credibility positive.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY — Medium-High

The screener shows beats in 7 of the last 7 quarters (100%), corroborated by the recurring "record [quarter] EPS" cadence and the third-quarter guidance raise. But two offsets: first, the $31B-to-$27B bookings miss is a real, named target that was missed; second, the "records" are flattered by buybacks and by an adjusted frame that excludes the very items (amortization, restructuring) that drove reported net income to $165M. There is a mild sandbagging pattern ("incremental conservatism" into the fourth-quarter 2025 print). On operational guidance (margin, currency, cost-out, deleveraging) reliability is high; on demand and bookings guidance it is medium. Net: medium-high.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **China growth-versus-market gap** (Colin Langan, on every call): "you were about 10% under market" — management repeatedly deflects to "customer mix," "Zeekr and NIO," and a promised normalization "by end of this year." It kept slipping; New Aptiv China was down 14% in the first quarter of 2026, with positive growth again promised "in Q2." Recurring and unresolved.
- **Tariffs, copper, Nexperia** — management consistently frames these as "manageable, pass-through, minor," and so far the financials do not contradict that.
- **Stranded costs and dis-synergies of the spin-off** (Picariello, fourth quarter of 2025): $70M stranded, $50M first-year drag for the remaining company, $15M of Versigent public-company costs — a real margin headwind analysts pushed on.
- **Intelligent Systems margin weakness** (Langan, fourth quarter of 2025): "surprisingly weak" margins; management attributes this to currency (down 170 basis points), engineering-credit timing, and stepped-up robotics investment that continues into 2026 — that is, a self-inflicted, ongoing margin headwind on the very segment meant to be the growth engine.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

Red flags. Reported earnings power collapsed — net income of $2.94B (2023), $1.79B (2024), $165M (2025); the "record EPS" story rests on adjusted figures plus a 20% lower share count, not rising real profit. Returns are below the cost of capital — return on invested capital 6.8%, net margin 0.8%; tier-1 auto economics with driver-assistance aspirations but commodity-supplier returns today. China is a persistent drag, not yet a driver — chronic below-market growth, program cancellations; the bullish "85% local-automaker bookings" has not converted to revenue acceleration. Insider conviction is negative — open-market only: 2 buys (9,800 shares) against 16 sells (116,488 shares); analyst price targets down 15%. The growth segment carries its own margin headwind — Intelligent Systems margins pressured by ongoing robotics investment.

Green flags. Deleveraging executed — the spin-off delivered $2.1B in cash, gross debt repaid, the remaining company at 1.9x net. Clean balance sheet. Disciplined, sustained buyback — about 20% of shares retired in a year; genuine per-share gains. Real cost-out — selling and administrative costs down 10% (2024), 5% targeted (2025), credible chief-financial-officer execution. Non-automotive and software mix-shift is structurally higher-margin — Wind River up more than 20%, non-automotive around $4B growing high-single to double digits; the right direction even if early. Portfolio simplified — the spin-off removes the lowest-margin, most-commoditized, copper- and peso-exposed segment, structurally lifting the remaining company's margins.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Segment rename** ("Advanced Safety & User Experience" becomes "Intelligent Systems," late 2025 into the first quarter of 2026) — a deliberate re-branding from auto-feature supplier to edge-intelligence and robotics platform, designed to argue for a tech multiple rather than an auto-parts multiple. Watch whether margins earn it.
- **Robotics as the new headline** — Robust AI, Vecna, Comau; Wind River's "$6B addressable market," the "$4,000-to-$5,000 content" comparison. This is the next narrative being seeded to replace Electrical Distribution Systems as the value driver. It is currently a cost (the fourth-quarter 2025 margin drag), not yet a revenue line.
- **Bookings target re-based** ($31B target, $27B actual, then more than $20B) and reframed so the lower absolute number looks like growth ("over $30B including Versigent" becomes "more than $20B New Aptiv").
- **The chief financial officer's currency candor** (peso hedging mechanics) is a credibility positive — management does not hide the headwind.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT — Pivoting (constructively)

Aptiv is turning: it has executed a clean, value-creating spin-off (Electrical Distribution Systems into Versigent, with the balance sheet de-levered to 1.9x net), it is re-branding the remainder toward higher-margin software, edge-intelligence, and robotics, and it is funding per-share gains with a disciplined buyback. The trajectory is constructive, but the new identity is promissory — the higher multiple it wants depends on a margin and return inflection that has not yet appeared in the numbers (return on invested capital 6.8%, net margin 0.8%, anemic 3.5% growth, China still negative).

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** the deep-value catalyst — the gap between market value and the separate worth of the parts — has already fired. The spin-off completed April 1, 2026; the $2.1B cash distribution is received and the debt is repaid. The input file's segment block (Electrical Distribution Systems at 41% of revenue) and net-debt figure ($6.18B) are stale, pre-spin numbers — any sum-of-the-parts that values that business as upside is double-counting an event that already happened. What remains is not a special situation but a fundamental bet on whether two higher-margin segments (Intelligent Systems plus Engineered Components) can re-rate from commodity-supplier returns (return on invested capital 6.8%) toward the tech multiple management is selling — against a 62% screener "shortfall" on the headline that is itself distorted by the collapsed 2025 reported net income.

**Economic moat:** the moat type is switching costs plus scale-driven cost advantages (multi-year design-in cycles, embedded harness, architecture and driver-assistance content, a supply-chain digital twin, Wind River intangibles). The moat is narrow but real. The evidence is in the returns: return on invested capital of 6.8% (below the cost of capital), net margin of 0.8%, gross margin expanding but off a low base — these are commodity tier-1 supplier returns, not a wide-moat franchise. Aptiv has real engineering know-how and customer entanglement, but pricing power is constrained by automaker cost pressure (the line "all the cost pressure going on in the industry is actually helpful to our business model" is the hope, not the demonstrated economics). The moat trend is stable to modestly widening in mix terms (the spin-off removes the most commoditized segment and tilts toward software and non-automotive), but returns have not yet inflected.

**Secular threat:** the relevant structural force is autonomous mobility and electrification of the drivetrain, and Aptiv sits on the right side of electric-vehicle content (high-voltage architecture, driver assistance, zonal compute) — more beneficiary than victim. The honest residual risk is commoditization of driver-assistance and compute under relentless automaker cost-down and Chinese local-supplier competition, plus cyclical auto-production exposure. This is manageable, not terminal — the long-run value is not structurally melting away (unlike a maker of internal-combustion-only parts); the risk is to margins, not to whether the demand exists at all.

A read on credibility: solid. Trajectory: turning. Moat: narrow but real. Moat trend: stable. Secular threat: manageable.
